abstract: This paper develops a new method for detecting coordinated link-sharing behavior on Facebook that goes beyond reliance on post-timing, which manipulators can easily alter. The authors exploit statistical regularities in the speed and frequency of link sharing across accounts to identify coordination. They validate the approach on a corpus of 11.2 million Facebook link posts drawn from roughly 16,000 sources.
key_claims: Existing coordination detection methods are vulnerable to evasion because they depend heavily on post-timing signals that are trivially manipulable.
Speed and frequency of link sharing exhibit highly regular statistical patterns that can serve as more robust indicators of coordination.
These statistical regularities can be operationalized into a detection method that improves on prior timing-based approaches.
methods: Analysis of a large-scale Facebook dataset comprising 11.2 million link posts.
Sourcing of posts from a curated list of approximately 16,000 accounts or domains.
Statistical modeling of sharing speed and frequency distributions to identify coordinated activity.
Empirical validation of the detection approach on the Facebook corpus.
findings: Link-sharing speed and frequency display consistent statistical regularities across accounts.
These regularities can be leveraged to detect coordinated sharing behavior on Facebook.
The approach is validated on a large empirical dataset of Facebook link posts.
contributions: A methodological advance in coordination detection that reduces dependence on easily manipulated post-timing features.
Introduction of speed- and frequency-based statistical signatures as detection signals.
Empirical application and validation at scale using millions of Facebook link posts.
framing: The paper situates itself within the literature on detecting coordinated inauthentic behavior and social media manipulation, responding to known weaknesses in timing-based coordination detection. It contributes to computational social science and platform integrity research by proposing more evasion-resistant detection signals.
